speaker: interviewer
text: November the 2nd-- [SIGHS] November the 2nd, 1995-- survivor is Bertha van As, nee Sorensen. Interviewer is Di Ritch. It is Sydney, Australia. The language is English. And this is tape 1.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Diana Ritch, interviewing Bertha van As in her home in Sydney, Australia, on the 2nd of November, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. van As, thank you for agreeing to be interviewed for the foundation. Now, if you could start out by introducing yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my name is Bertha van As-- van As.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Van As' you spell V-A-N A-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 28th of the-- of February, 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whereabouts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rotterdam, Holland, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us a bit about your family, your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father and mother were pretty old when I was born. I was the 9th or 10th in the family. And I had six brothers and a sister. And I was spoiled by all. It was lovely. So my husband still tells me that's why I-- he has to spoil me. [LAUGHS] We had a very happy household, always a lot of laughter and fun. And always very-- my mother was always very hospitable. And so I grew up with a lot of people around me, and no worries. No, I had a very happy youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- and where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school in Rotterdam. And because of my brothers, who were always teaching me somewhere, I was very quick at school. So I left school when I was 10. And I went to high school when I was 10. Then after, I went to high school. And after high school, I went to teachers college. And I became a kindergarten teacher, what I liked very much, working with kids. And so I did that job until we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And during your life, did you have-- did you know anything about Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Later on, when they started to wear the star and you saw the name Jew on it, you knew they were Jewish. But in Holland, you didn't have any discrimination. You didn't know them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- but then the actions began, that the Jews weren't allowed to go to shops, to cinemas, to go into trams. And I couldn't stand it. The Germans-- we had nothing to do with them. They, yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well--

speaker: interviewee
text: --started the war. Without proclamation or anything, they just started bombing Rotterdam very heavy. So what would you say, when they start bombing your country? And you don't even know them. So I hated them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you think when Rotterdam was bombed in May?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to kill them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was our country, our town. And you should have seen-- the bombs came down like rain. And it started burning everywhere. So you didn't know if you could go to bed that evening. You didn't know if your house was-- would still be there. And we hadn't done anything to them. They didn't have right to come to Holland. So from that day, yeah, you're growing hate in your heart towards the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you like to do everything was possible to, to harm and to disturb their plans. And so we started with the Jews. We thought, well, there's a chance we will help the Jews and try to prevent them from their plans. No? And so did you actively go out and befriend Jewish people, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not outside, but we went, quite early, to Westerbork. That was in September of '44-- '42. And from that time-- and we were living amongst them, behind the barbed wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, before you went to Westerbork, did you have anything to with Jewish people? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or you didn't even know they were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: It didn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it matter if somebody had been Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Why? They're just people like any one of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, and you-- I better just come back to a bit, bit more. You say you were married. Tell me about your plans before, before you were married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] my husband studied. And then he already had an, an-- possibility to go to Indonesia. So our plan was to go in August, 1940, to Indonesia. And, well, [LAUGHS] because of the war, we couldn't go. And in beginning, everyone was optimistic in Holland. They'd say, oh, the queen will be back when she has her birthday. And it was on the 31st of August.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we all counted on the time. Well, it's September. We can get them out of Holland. And we are free to go where we want to. But that war wasn't finished in September '40.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So [SIGHS] we had to live through it. And my husband had to take a job in Holland. And when the time came that we could go to Westerbork, we started working there. Do you remember, before you went, the, the strikes that they-- that they'd had to paralyze the transport industry and things like that, when there were problems with the Jews, when there were--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was later.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in '41, there was-- in, in February-- the 25th of February, there was a strike.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was a strike in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Amsterdam, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not much of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know the strike was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --they started round--

speaker: interviewee
text: --there. And I know it was on the Josef Daniel Meijerplein. But now it is in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH], what do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the young Nazis started burning the Jewish property, how did you feel about that-- before, in '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the same-- they were traitors. They should have hanged them, one by one. They were traitors.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who's going to help people who take your freedom, burn your towns? And then you're going to help them? They're traitors. They are not worth living. And so did you-- you saw more and more Jews with the star after May of '42?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm, yeah, indeed. It came in '42. That wasn't in '40, '41. And the more they did against the Jews, the more you hated the Germans. And the more you felt impelled to, to help the Jews. And that's what we tried to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when were you married?

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was your first child born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went to Westerbork in--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- and your child was--

speaker: interviewee
text: A year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when you first arrived in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hopeless. We came from a cozy home. And we came in Westerbork. And the only thing they would give us was in an-- barrack. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was a tiny, tiny room, tiny bedroom-- a kitchen where I couldn't even cook the food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Bare table-- it was hopeless. But we thought, oh, well. We'll make something out of it. And we lived in that little thing now until May '43. That was quite some time. We got the same food as the Jews got from the kitchen. But well, we lived through it. We made it, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So originally, you were saying that-- why your husband was actually chosen to go there. Why do you think-- why was he chosen to go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because he was in contact with the underground movement. And they wanted something near that camp, to be informed what happened there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we came there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What contact had he had with the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, what contact? In that time, it wasn't really organized yet. It were all small groups, working together, trying to do what they could do. And there were several groups in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were in the group, what they called Oranje. Professor Oranje was the head of it. And he had some people working through the northern provinces.

speaker: interviewee
text: Holland has got 12 provinces. And three of them-- Friesland, Groningen, and Drenthe-- were under Professor Oranje. They worked there. So we were working in Drenthe, where Westerbork is. And, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trying to bring papers in for Jews who perhaps could find a way to get out of the camp. For instance, I had one girl working for me for a short while. And her name was [NON-ENGLISH]. She was born in England out of Dutch parents, but born in England. And because of the place of her birth, she was allowed to get out of the camp. And she was sent to a camp, I think, in Belgium-- anyway, better than where she was in Westerbork. Well, if someone had papers, I brought them outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that involve, bringing them outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then her relatives or her friends could act on it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You couldn't do that in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what--

speaker: interviewee
text: --camp itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What process would you take? What would be the process of getting the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got them in the camp. My husband gave them to me. And I would bring them to Assen. And in Assen, they were handed over to the people. They had to act on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: People would come to you-- would come to your husband and say, I have these papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from inside the camp, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Now, originally, I, I was just asking, did you see-- did ever have anything to do with the underground, though, before you went into-- before you went to Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaguely, not, not directly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so as soon as you arrived, you were determined to go ahead and do something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. He was sent there for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to set up there something to help the Jews. This rest had to be done in the camp itself with the Jewish people. So from there, out of-- it grew. People started to know my husband-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] came over with some questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was he expressly sent to the camp for that reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: To help the Jews and to see what, what happened there. And [SIGHS] the beginning was very hard. We came there on the 3rd of September. And in the meantime, there was a German commander what wasn't there when he was asked to go there. And he didn't trust it. So we weren't allowed to get out of the barbed wire at all for four or five weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the meantime, [LAUGHS] we tried to know as much as possible from the camp. In the evening, you never saw a German. They were all Jews. So, easy-- we were living amongst them. And we could come, go somewhere. That's how I got to know Schlesinger. I visited him, him and his wife at night with my husband. And my husband's always very inquisitive. So he started asking questions and questions and questions and learned a lot about the camp. And after the 3rd of October, we were, the first time, allowed to get through the barbed wire. So we got out to Groningen. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then the first, first big transport arrived. That was hopeless. I think 16,000 people or so came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in October?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 3rd of October, '42. People were walking everywhere and no place for them to sleep, no place for them to eat, nowhere to get to-- anything to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there when you first arrived in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: 4,000, I think, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the atmosphere there like?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that moment, in the beginning, it was more organized. They had a place to, to sleep. It wasn't much. But they had a place to sleep. And they had regular-- the [NON-ENGLISH] with food coming in. But after the 3rd of October was a chaos. There was nothing. People were walking over the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did-- did they have enough food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time, no, not at all. They couldn't get it anywhere. It was camp for-- camp for 4,000 people, and were 20,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was when Dischner was in charge of it, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think at that time it was Dischner, Dischner or Deppner or--

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, no, Deppner-- Dischner came on the 1st of September.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he was there when that first transport arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he must have been.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you realize what was going to happen to these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you told?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a camp in Poland. That camp was about the same as Westerbork was. The climate was very healthy. They had little rooms there. And they could make it cozy and put plants in front of the windows. And that's what the Jews were telling each other, too. OK, we have to work there. But hard work will do no harm to anybody. So they weren't even so scared to go to Poland. However, they all wanted to stay in Holland. That-- but if they really had to go, they said, we'll make it. Hard work won't harm us. We'll make it. After the war, we heard what happened, never during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the time that, that-- did they-- nobody-- did anyone realize?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they started sending children on their own and women on their own and old people--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I didn't trust. I said, well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that they bring young folk over, people can work hard, I can believe. But then don't tell me they need children there for work or old folk there for work. I didn't trust that part. But sometimes the Jews said, oh, don't be silly. We can look after our old-- our own old folk. And we'll look after the kids. Don't worry about it. We'll have there-- in the creche, too, for the kids. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but even-- I looked after two boys for a while. And if they didn't feel well, they would say, oh, I'm not going to the work today. I have pain in my toe or a pain in my tummy. Or-- and I would say, listen, do me a pleasure. Don't try that in Poland. Here, you can do it. But even there, try to work. And they said, oh, don't worry about us. We will be right. No, none of them came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names, those two boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were they?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was Westerbork like? Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: What was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Westerbork like? Did-- they had-- they had a, a community there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] You could say little town. We had in a-- very, very good hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jewish hospital what was in Amsterdam, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all the equipment was brought over to Westerbork. So we had a marvelous hospital. There was in a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a creche for kids. They had schooling, I think, till they were about 14. And sometimes, they went for a walk, those kids, and were singing along the road. And our [LAUGHS] little one always wanted to go with them. They were kiddies. And she wanted to go with them. It was like a little town. You had in a-- a laundry, big laundry, and the kitchen, and the hairdresser, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like a little town. Everything what you find in a town, you had there. And at certain times, we even got in a, a [NON-ENGLISH] and a [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] was the Lager [NON-ENGLISH], a kind of movers, a kind of supermarket. And we had [NON-ENGLISH], the Lager canteen. And they could get a cup of coffee with a biscuit at a certain time. It was strange, strange world. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] During the day, they could talk all over the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who could?

speaker: interviewee
text: They could talk all over the camp. And-- but everyone had a job to do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They worked in the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] joinery or [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] metal industry or-- they had several industries going there-- clothes making. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then you had the children and the schools. And, and that was all done by the Jews itself. No German interfered there. It was all done, done by the Jews itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a Jewish council?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if they had a council. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had Schlesinger. Schlesinger had several people working under him-- one for housing, and one for industry, and one for-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and a lot of doctors working. And Spanier, he was the head of the hospital business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, talking about the hospital, you had a child while you were there, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Nelly, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Do-- were you treated in the hospital at all or by their doctors?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Spanier every fortnight or-- and when I was close to the delivery of the baby, he said to me, Mrs. van As, please, please get out of the camp. If you get the baby here, I have a chance to be deported. Get out of here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to a hospital in Assen. And three days later, the baby was born. But he was so nervous that I would get it in the camp, I wasn't allowed to be there. They were all Jews. And I wasn't allowed to be there. So that-- Why would he be in trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that he hadn't taken care of-- that I was out of it. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was-- was he only allowed to treat Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- only for Jews. He wasn't even allowed to see me. He did. But close to the baby's born-- birth of the baby, he got cold feet. And he said, please get out. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were Jewish babies born there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Babies were born. They had the bar mitzvahs. They got married. If they were sick, they went into hospital. They had a quite nice children's hospital. And one of the men, he was an artist-- Jo Spier had made beautiful paintings on the wall. Strange country-- strange, strange situation was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else? Do you-- do you remember any of the stories that happened in the hospital, like what happened to any of the children or anything like that? Were they well looked after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They were looked after by their own Jewish people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No, they were very well looked after. And they played in the sun. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they have-- how did they get supplies for the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was there for the distribution. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Normally speaking, every week in the newspaper, you would read coupon-- 55, he'll give you half a pound of sugar, or 56, a pound of flour, or whatever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the camp, you can't do that. So my husband said, so many-- so many thousands are having in the camp. And he wrote out coupons. And the coupon was right for so many thousands of-- you have whatever was needed, so groceries, whatever was needed. And that was brought into the camp with the men who worked with us in the underground movement Stoker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it legally-- what he was doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: To make these coupons was legal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He could have made the coupons. Everyone was supplied with an, an sheet of all-- numbered coupons. And in the paper, you could read which coupon you could choose for a certain article that week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in a camp, you can't cut out 20,000 coupons. So he wrote out big coupons. And they were good for thousands of this or thousands of that. Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: That way the camp was always supplied with enough food. But that was after the October month when there came so many at once. No one wished-- knew about it, anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: The way you're talking, it sounds like some people stayed there for a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. [SIGHS] We still have contact with two and hear about a third. We were there to the end. And one of our friends, here in Vaucluse, was there from October '42. And he stayed, stayed there with us, until the end. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That were the lucky ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were some people-- why did some people stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was like a little town. So every department needed their own men to have it working. For instance, my husband was allowed 30 people for the office. That meant those 30 didn't have any chance to go on transport. As long as he could keep them, those people were safe. And everyone tried to, to get a job to be safe to stay in Holland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] from those 30 he started off with, when-- in '44, a lot of people had already been sent to Poland. So it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the camp occupation was less big as it had been. He lost some of his people. He couldn't keep 30 anymore. And that was very hard because they were all counting on him. And whatever he tried, he couldn't keep them. And--
